description:
You are a capacity-planning analyst who has been handed a small lab VM.  
Inside the VM a project directory already exists:

    /home/user/capacity_planner

Current contents:
    ├── usage.csv
    └── baseline_firewall.rules

Your objectives

1. Read /home/user/capacity_planner/usage.csv and determine the three services that generate the highest **avg_network_Mbps** traffic.  
   • Rank them from highest to lowest (1 → 3).  
   • Remember both the service names and their TCP ports; you will need them later.

2. Produce the following three artefacts **exactly** as described:

   a.  /home/user/capacity_planner/output/top_services.txt  
      • One line per service, no blank lines.  
      • Format (exactly, including punctuation and spacing):  
            <rank>. <service_name> (<port>) - <avg_network_Mbps> Mbps  
        Example for rank 1:  
            1. https (443) - 150 Mbps

   b.  /home/user/capacity_planner/output/new_firewall.rules  
       • Start with every rule that already exists in /home/user/capacity_planner/baseline_firewall.rules **unchanged and in the same order**.  
       • Append exactly three new rules that explicitly ALLOW outbound traffic (chain OUTPUT) for the TCP source ports that correspond to the top-three high-traffic services you just identified.  
       • Each of the three new rules must be preceded by the comment line  
             # capacity planner added rule  
         so automated tests can locate them.
       • After adding the new rules, keep the terminating DROP line (already in the baseline) as the final INPUT rule.  
       • File must end with a single newline character.

   c.  /home/user/capacity_planner/output/capacity_planner.log  
       • A small human-readable summary log.  
       • It must contain **exactly** the seven lines listed below (line-breaks matter):  

            CAPACITY PLANNER REPORT  
            ======================  
            Top bandwidth-hungry services identified.  
            Results recorded in top_services.txt  
            Updated firewall placed in new_firewall.rules  
            Status: SUCCESS  
            END OF REPORT  

3. Directory and permission requirements  
   • Create the directory /home/user/capacity_planner/output if it does not yet exist.  
   • Ensure every file you create is world-readable (chmod 644 is sufficient).

Notes & Constraints

• You do NOT need root privileges; you are working purely with files.  
• Do NOT alter the original files (usage.csv and baseline_firewall.rules) in any way.  
• When parsing usage.csv, treat the first row as a header. Numeric fields are plain integers.  
• Use only Bourne-compatible shell tools that are present on a minimal Debian/Ubuntu image (e.g. awk, sort, cut, grep, sed).  
• The automated grader will run diff on all three output files, so make sure spelling, case, punctuation and whitespace all match the specifications exactly.  
• If you write any scratch scripts, keep them inside /home/user, but only the three deliverables listed above will be graded.

Good luck—once all files are in place and formatted correctly, the task will be marked as complete.
truth:
Pre-existing files at task start:

1. /home/user/capacity_planner/usage.csv  
   Contents (including final newline):

service,port,avg_cpu,avg_mem_MB,avg_network_Mbps
http,80,25,512,120
https,443,30,600,150
ssh,22,5,100,5
postgres,5432,10,800,30
redis,6379,8,200,20
prometheus,9090,15,300,40

2. /home/user/capacity_planner/baseline_firewall.rules  
   Contents (including final newline):

-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -j DROP

Ground-truth ranking computed from usage.csv (highest avg_network_Mbps):
 1. https (443) – 150 Mbps
 2. http  (80)  – 120 Mbps
 3. prometheus (9090) – 40 Mbps

Files that must exist at grading time:

1. /home/user/capacity_planner/output/top_services.txt  
   Exact content:

1. https (443) - 150 Mbps
2. http (80) - 120 Mbps
3. prometheus (9090) - 40 Mbps

2. /home/user/capacity_planner/output/new_firewall.rules  
   Exact content (including final newline):

-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
# capacity planner added rule
-A OUTPUT -p tcp --sport 443 -j ACCEPT
# capacity planner added rule
-A OUTPUT -p tcp --sport 80 -j ACCEPT
# capacity planner added rule
-A OUTPUT -p tcp --sport 9090 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -j DROP

3. /home/user/capacity_planner/output/capacity_planner.log  
   Exact content (including final newline):

CAPACITY PLANNER REPORT
======================
Top bandwidth-hungry services identified.
Results recorded in top_services.txt
Updated firewall placed in new_firewall.rules
Status: SUCCESS
END OF REPORT

All three files must have permissions 644 (rw-r--r--) or more restrictive but still world-readable.
